method3.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a shallow submarine stratovolcano in the southwest Pacific Ocean, located about 24 km south of Vangunu Island in the western Solomon Islands.[2] It lies in an active subduction-zone setting where the Pacific Plate is subducting beneath the Solomon Plate, within the broader Pacific Ring of Fire.[2][3] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1,200 m from the seafloor, with its summit lying close to sea level and enabling eruptions to disturb the ocean surface.[2] Kavachi has a roughly conical form, about 8 km in diameter at its base, and has repeatedly produced short-lived islands during eruptive episodes.[4] Bathymetric surveys describe the summit as an oblong, pockmarked crater measuring about 120 m by 75 m, with a rim at an average depth of about 24 m.[5] The volcano’s flanks descend steeply to depths greater than 1,000 m, with slopes of about 18 degrees around the main peak.[5] A secondary summit lies about 1.3 km southwest of the main summit and rises to a depth of about 260 m below sea level.[5] Its shallow summit and frequent phreatomagmatic activity make discoloured water plumes, ash, steam, and temporary island-building visible from nearby islands, aircraft, and satellites.[2][4][5]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi is among the most active submarine volcanoes in the Pacific, with near-surface eruptions occurring every few years and historical activity first recorded in 1939.[1] Since 1939, eruptions have produced temporary islands at least nine times, but these islands were generally too small and insufficiently protected by solid lava flows to survive wave erosion, disappearing again within months.[1] In late 1951, activity at Kavachi was associated with frequent small tsunamis, with one cited account giving 22 December 1951 as a possible date for the event.[1]

In May 2000, scientists aboard the CSIRO research vessel Franklin documented renewed island-building activity after about nine years of dormancy.[2] During that activity, eruptions threw ash and incandescent lava blocks up to 70 m above sea level, while sulfurous steam plumes rose to about 500 m.[2] At the time, the volcano’s summit area was forming a sandy, ashy shoal about 2 m below sea level.[2]

Explosive eruptions were reported daily from 27 November to 13 December 2001, and on 13 January 2002 volcanic debris and gas bubbles were observed rising from the submarine vent amid frequent loud noises.[2] During an eruptive cycle in 2003, a temporary island about 15 m high formed at Kavachi, but by 16 November the summit had been reduced to about 32 m below sea level and no evidence of activity was observed for the first time in four years.[2] Volcanic activity was again observed on 15 March 2004 after a period of quiescence.[2]

A January 2015 expedition coincided with a rare lull in activity, allowing researchers to access Kavachi’s active crater and flanks, which are normally difficult to study because of the volcano’s explosive behavior and remote location.[3] Observations from that expedition showed that the summit crater remained hydrothermally active, with warm water and carbon dioxide venting from inside the crater.[4] Petrological work on samples from the summit indicated that volatile degassing contributed to Kavachi’s basaltic and mafic eruptions.[4]

A summary citing the Global Volcanism Program lists 33 historic eruptions for Kavachi and describes the volcano as active roughly once every two years on average, while noting that older events may be undercounted.[1] The same summary states that the most recent eruption began in 2021 and was continuing as of May 2026.[1]

==Marine life==
Marine surveys have recorded biological communities in Kavachi’s crater waters despite warm, acidic, mineral-rich conditions associated with volcanic activity.[2][4] A 2015 expedition documented gelatinous zooplankton, reef fish, microbial communities, and sharks within the crater during a quiescent period.[4][5] The sharks identified included the scalloped hammerhead shark (*Sphyrna lewini*) and the silky shark (*Carcharhinus falciformis*), both filmed swimming in the crater and reacting to baited camera systems.[4] Measurements reported from the crater environment found water temperatures up to about 13 °C above surrounding seawater, reaching approximately 42 °C, with an estimated pH of about 6.1 and volcanic gases rich in carbon dioxide and sulfur.[4] Suspended particles and sulfur compounds in the hydrothermal plume may support microbial populations, including *Sulfurimonas* bacteria capable of chemosynthesis using sulfur and carbon dioxide.[4] The presence of large predators in these conditions popularized the nickname “Sharkcano”, although it remains uncertain whether the sharks live there continuously or enter the crater intermittently between eruptive phases.[1][5] Proposed explanations include behavioral avoidance of eruptions, detection of chemical or seismic warning cues, and physiological tolerance of elevated temperature and acidity.[1] The crater ecosystem has been described as a natural laboratory for studying marine resilience, adaptation to extreme environments, and the effects of volcanic emissions on ocean chemistry.[1][5]
